The kind of stupid fake RPG people that don't actually like RPGs like. Terrible RPG mechanics with big skill trees that just change very little. This is a game with cybernetic implants yet there's so little ability to meaningfully change or augment your character. So much is just % stat modifiers and it's all so bland and creatively bankrupt.

The game creates a narrative of urgency, only to drop you into an open world encouraging you to waste as much time as possible in Night City. This kind of gameplay-narrative dissonance really takes away from the storytelling, in my opinion. The story itself seems decent enough, there's good voice acting and facial animations, but the game refuses to break first person which can make the storytelling feel stiff and limited by this decision. I find it hard to immerse when protagonist V just feels like a camera & voicebox watching people talk rather than a living part of the world.

Combat is very bland & repetitive and there is just so much of it to get through. Driving is the absolute basic level of serviceable. Character customisation is okay, but again it's largely first-person so it feels a lot like fluff. Music was really not for me. Graphics are mostly great though.

This is a crowd-pleasing 'RPG' for those that just want to mindlessly kill things and experience a drawn out story, but for me it all just feels so empty and soulless despite trying to like it.

If Red Dead Redemption is the best game franchise ever, this is the second best.
I only wish there were deeper and more variety of interactions.
Legendary game overall.

Growing up I always wanted to play an open-world game that felt convincing enough, that I could really get into character. For me, Cyberpunk 2077 is the first time I truly experienced that. I know not everyone loves how combat feels, but to me, the story richness and atmosphere is the strength of the game. I enjoy driving to my destinations, getting distracted by things and listening to the radio; I enjoy the slow passage of time and pretty to-scale map distances -- they make the world and my time playing feel "full". I'm not an FPS gamer, and I struggle with shooting games but to me, combat feels manageable at the Hard difficulty, and there are many ways to approach each scenario you may land yourself in, which really helps me not feel railroaded at any given point, and contributes to the game as a roleplaying experience. If this type of reasoning makes sense to you, get the game. You'll probably enjoy it as much as I did.

Cyberpunk 2077 is one of the best games ever made. The story is truly unique and you really feel for the characters. The voice actors did a fantastic job especially male V. While some prefer the female performance, I found the male voice to be incredibly intimidating and impactful.

The gameplay is top-notch. You can play differently every single run, and even repeating a build never feels boring. However, even with the freedom of the skill tree, some dialogue options or outcomes are blocked by the level cap. Even with mods, you can’t max out everything, which is one of the game's few downsides.

Night City feels unique. Even without the random world events found in games like Red Dead Redemption 2, the side gigs provide plenty of content, offering at least 40 hours of playtime on a first run. The endings are also stellar; each one is distinct and worth experiencing.

The romance options feel genuine because they require significant investment unlike The Witcher 3, where some encounters feel more random. Furthermore, the Phantom Liberty DLC improves on the base game's missions significantly. It’s clear CDPR wanted to create the best expansion possible.

If you have a decent rig, this game is absolutely worth buying. It remains one of the best-looking titles on the market and a staple for hardware benchmarks.

10/10

I waited to write this review to let time pass, observe mechanical updates, and distance myself from real-world events I experienced during my first playthrough. My conclusions remain unchanged. I played on release via Stadia (logging around 600 hours, bug-free) and later transitioned to Steam with the expansion. 

If you are looking for a highly structured, immersive world where you can exist as a single character, this game delivers. But beneath the surface, it is a heavy, uncompromising examination of autonomy, mental health, and the ethical cost of survival.

## Fair content warning: ##  This review contains spoilers and discusses severe mental health issues and suicide.

## THE WORLD AND THE ATMOSPHERE

As someone who grew up in the 80s, the retro-future aesthetic resonates well with me. The environmental design — ranging from corporate skyscrapers to neon-lit, trash-filled alleys — balances surface splendour with the tragedy of an unforgiving dystopia. The soundscape is very good, and the OST remains in my regular rotation. I particularly liked the diegetic inclusion of music through radios, speakers, etc. It feels natural. 

The developers successfully created a graphically believable universe with its own distinct clothing, colloquialisms, and professions.

On the downside, I wish they had done something like the procedural, emergent roleplay of games like *Daggerfall*. The city is dense, but the NPCs lack complex schedules or interactions. Most of the world is setting, and there are few things (or people!) you can interact with. It was a satisfying (but frustrating) realization to find out you could actually confess yourself in the absolution vending machines.

There is also a distinct tension  — and this is a crucial issue  — between exploring the world and the main quest. The narrative insists V is dying rapidly, urging you to rush. It makes absolute sense, but doing so deprives you of exploring the setting. But if you suspend the dissonance this urgency creates, the lore and visual storytelling are highly detailed.

## GAMEPLAY AND MECHANICS

The gunplay and movement mechanics are satisfying, offering varied approaches: fast-paced reflexes, time-dilating cyberware, or real-time quickhacks. The combat functions well, accommodating everything from stealth to direct assault.

However, the RPG elements lack structural consequence. The reputation system is shallow: working for one gang should logically anger others, and aiding the police should make you a target in the underworld. This all could lead tor restricting access to certain resources or quests based on alliances, and would foster replayability.

Similarly, the game allows complete character respecs. I value games where you can make your own choices, but choices must have some degree of consequence, and this applies also to character creation and development. A system where choices have permanence — where mastering hacking precludes being a swordmaster, for instance — would ground the experience. 

The game also omits cyberpsychosis as a gameplay mechanic for the player. Lore dictates that heavy bodily modification risks mental breakdown, yet the game imposes no such penalty on V. This is ironic because cyberpsychosis is hardly forgotten in these modern iterations of the setting: the entire Edgerunners series runs on it. It is also (in the TTRPG) a very important mechanic to limit the possibilitites of expanding your own character or respeccing it.

These issues I am critiquing are not a problem for Cyberpunk 2077 only — this plague is an AAA development philosophy hesitant to lock players out of content, but in doing so, robs them of actual consequence to their actions.

## NARRATIVE, MENTAL HEALTH, AND ENDINGS

Yet, apart from my appreciation of its lore and aesthetics, where the game holds the most value for me is in its somber handling of psychological trauma and survival. I have seen other players too credit this game's themes with helping them process dark times in their own lives, and looking at the quests, one can imagine why. 

The game repeatedly forces engagement with autonomy and personhood: The Delamain Arc requires permanent choices regarding a fractured AI's perception of reality. Brendan is vending machine treated as corporate property — he insists he is not sentient, yet acts as if he is, raising practical questions about the definition of personhood. Lizzy Wizzy is clearly suffering from cyberpsychosis, but also facing the horror of having her mind tampered with by a partner. Barry's story is an approach to depression from a darkly humorous angle, but delivers a grim look at how mental health struggles are ignored by peers.

Cyberpsycho sightings themselves, on which I haven't seen much comment, are excuses for boss fights, ok. But if you look through them, they are tragedies. The one titled 'Family Matters' hit very hard, with one woman struggling to help her sister, and ultimately losing her husband and even her life to it.

People often cite that the main narrative is 'V is dying', but that isn't accurate: the body can survive. V is facing her mind being rewritten by the Relic, and that is a much more horrible reality. I think the game does a good job at presenting this, both subtly and explicitly: V's dialogue options and actions (like smoking) begin to mirror Johnny Silverhand, and Johnny's own arc shifts as he realizes this, moving from hostility to empathy. You are fading away, and the game acknowledges the terror of that process.

## CONTENT WARNING: SUICIDE DISCUSSION

During my first playthrough, I was dealing with the immediate aftermath of a suicide attempt by someone close to me. I interrupted the attempt, but witnessing their intense pain left me grappling with whether I did them a disservice. I prioritize personal autonomy, and intervening felt like an intrusion on their ultimate choice, even if it stemmed from a compromised mental state.

Processing the game through that lens influenced how I saw the final choices. Players often view the endings as a way to «win» but, to me, they were a calculation of whom I was willing to sacrifice. Asking the Nomads for help gets many of them killed to buy you time. Siding with Rogue gets her killed. Siding with Arasaka surrenders both your mind and Johny's to them. The secret «solo» ending requires the mass murder of corporate guards who are ultimately just working people.

Under this light, the suicide ending seems the cleanest, most ethical option. It respects the autonomy and safety of everyone else. You prioritize your friends' lives above your survival, deciding not to drag them into your self-destruction. Johnny respects the choice. Your friends will call in the credits, grieving and cursing you, but the structural fact remains: you did not force anyone else to pay the price for your life.

## CONCLUSION

I acknowledge my experience with this game comes from a very specific, difficult place in my life, which colored my perception. Mechanically, it has flaws and missed opportunities for deeper RPG consequences. However, it captures the genre's aesthetic accurately and delivers a solid narrative. If you are willing to engage with its themes of autonomy, mental health, and the systemic cost of survival, I think it offers a dense, worthwhile experience.

The NPCs are arguably the game’s weakest point; they simply wander the city aimlessly.  Conversations with them are limited and lack proper reactions.  While the story is decent, I haven’t finished it yet but it’ll keep you entertained.

My personal gripes for disliking the game are as follows: The way it is designed means you can do any kind of run or build an all around character and let me tell you the content and world building is to die for. But it begins to fall flat when there is not much variety in weapons due to them just being scaled into tiers of 1-5. This was a great RPG to play but there is no pacing with the side quests and a lot of the purchases you make can be avoided and you can just focus on getting Cybernetic augments and that becomes the game as become and evolved lifeform. You can ignore hacking, you can ignore melee combat you can ignore being a stealth build. Which is fine and proper but once you find a gameplay style that works for you the entire game stops feeling fun and its just a "go here and do this, kill x amount of people, get paid."

Still one hell of a story, one I admire but you see the flaws. V's story is one where there are no good endings only what you willing to sacrifice to go big or die in an unmarked grave. Once you put speed bumps on the story I find you can easily break this the perk systems so your playstyle moves from FPS to mass murdering simulation. Good if you want that but I honestly wish I knew more going into the Cyberpunk universe. Its a very old RPG and the California setting really did not to it must justice if you ask me.

I adored the characters and the journey. Hell I almost 200 hours into it. But its so good it misses out in other areas and many of the character customization features are designed for looks not for real immersion. Sure its got quality but to me its just another grind fest where you can ignore all the side quests and till have a fine time. That is a red flag for me. I have always been a fan of CD Projekt Red but trying to do a dystopian rogue AI landscape works except there are no monsters and the ruins of civilization for a corporate supremacy becomes a boring environment quickly. I felt drained and disappointed after completing the main missions in both Vanilla and Phantom Liberty because nothing improves in the world its just you signing off permanetly what is a well written world where technology replaces a good degree of mythology but so much is missing.

I would have loved to see real mutants or hybrids which do exist lore wise in the game but again, so much time and money was spent on making a high fi retro vibe for all designs and culture you see inside of 2077 it removes everything else. You remain a boring human with boring yet flashy character designs or customizations to your body but they really do nothing. You are in a vice grip of conflict that coexists such interesting character story arc's that once you realize not many of them end well the experience becomes draining but a ride worth taking once. Repeated playthroughs with making different choices offer some thrill and then its gone. Violently, you are left looking for the next thrill, firefight or something you didn't discover. Like the desert of the Mohave, this place dries you up fast. 

You are in for a wild ride but don't expect any rewards long term. Feels good to become a badass and literally become the center of attention but how that plays into exploring anything beyond that? Its a hollow shell like those drained oil fields or trash heaps, and the world becomes very boring. I give them a 9/10 but personally the more I play and beat the game over and over again, the more I dislike 2077 with a heavy heart. I would have liked to see a game modeled after the original source material this RPG was molded by decades ago. But alas, my purist pursuits of old world RPGs and their iconic names means this game could never have lived long to my expectations. 

Still I was fine paying 60$ for it, the quality and content is there but once you milk it all.....don't expect it to give anything back with interest. I am happy to say now I know what the hype is about. CD Projekt Red can make a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ killer story which remains emotionally charged and damaging. I just wish there was more options and variety. I don't hate this game but I expected more when it came to roleplaying. This plays like "Doomplaying" given how your main character is running out of time regardless of what happens.

There are games you enjoy, and then there are games that stay with you long after the credits roll. Cyberpunk doesn’t deal in fairy tales. It deals in consequences. In ambition. In that relentless human drive to survive, no matter the cost. At its core, this story is about the will to keep going. About how far someone will push, who they’ll become, and what they’re willing to sacrifice to leave their mark. It’s about blurred lines where ends start to justify the means and the constant reminder that in a world like this, there are no happy endings. Wrong city, wrong people. Cyberpunk 2077 isn’t perfect. But it doesn’t need to be. It’s human. And that’s what makes it unforgettable.

Well, this game has a long history. Starting with 'overpromised and underdelivered' unto a very decent RPG which was not and is not the first next-gen game of its kind, but the last old-gen game of its kind. If you can live with that: Play it. But the game was best with all those hilarious initial bugs. I liked "Cyberbug 1.06" best - I still have that version on one of my hard drives. In one quest I parked my car outside of the mission building, did the quest and heard an ear-shattering sound, as if a bomb had exploded. I had just finished the quest and a delivery vehicle for the quest item spawned inside my own vehicle, demolishing it to smithereens. I could not enter my car and I could not deliver the quest item. It took the 'AI' hours on end to clear the rubble and finally I could finish the quest. It was so good. One more note: There is a quest-line called "Sinnerman", one of my favourite quests of all time. Play the game just for this quest, it could only originate in a land like Poland with a strong Catholic tradition. The quest was absolutely mind-blowing and deeply disturbing. Both - failed quest and good quest - are highlights in my gaming life diary. An easy recommendation.

Cyberpunk 2077 is a breathtaking experience, visually, mechanically, and narratively. This is my second time 100% completing the game (first on Xbox, now post-Cyberpunk 2077: Phantom Liberty), and it still manages to pull me in in a way very few games can.

The jank and existential threat to your sanity that came with launch? Long gone. What remains is a genuinely polished, deeply immersive masterpiece that leaves you wanting more and maybe questioning your life choices along the way.

The base game alone delivers a thrilling story, packed with hours of side content, gigs, and jobs to keep any merc busy. The characters are compelling, and Night City feels truly alive… even if it’s mostly chrome, neon, and bad decisions. Since the 2.0 update, the game feels like an entirely new experience compared to my original playthrough, smoother, deeper, and far more engaging.

And then there’s Phantom Liberty, which is not just the icing on the cake, but the icing, the cherry, and the entire bakery. It’s that good. The expansion constantly shifts your focus, subverts expectations, and keeps you in a state of tension where you’re never quite sure who you can trust. Who’s really your choom? Honestly… probably no one.

The writing is exceptional. It takes the already strong character work from the base game and pushes it even further, diving into themes of loyalty, control, and survival. By the end, the idea of a “good” ending feels almost irrelevant, because in Night City, there really aren’t any. Not for V, and not for the people they care about.

I won’t spoil anything, but this game sticks with you. The sounds, the atmosphere, the skyline, it all lingers long after you log off.

And yeah… you are breathtaking.

Despite the long production time, the many delays, and the launch that was good but hype and entitlement held up to an unrealistic standard, this is truly a masterpiece. One of the greatest games ever made. 

Based on the tabletop rpg from the 80's and insanely faithful to the lore of that setting and world. It's easy to see that CD Projekt Red are genuine fans of the source material. 

Night City feels alive, it feels real, and after a while it feels like home. A dysfunctional and messy home but a home nonetheless. 

 The characters have nuance and multitudes to their personalities and it never feels like they're waiting around for the player to show up. 

The story is one for the ages that explores themes of death, immortality, justice and vengeance. 

The addition of the numerous updates that smooth out the early kinks, as references to the show and ttrpg as well as the DLC, Phantom Liberty, only add a pristine cherry on top of the experience. Phantom Liberty keeps the developers tradition alive of expansions truly worthy of the base game. 

If you enjoy gaming at all as an entertainment and storytelling medium then this is a must play.

Before anything, I am saying this now: I will be spoiling the endings. And probably more of the story. I am also saying this: I played the game with some mods, and on the latest version, 2.31, with PL installed.

I didn't know what to expect out of Cyberpunk. Maybe an open-world game with RPG systems and semi-futuristic guns with a bunch of chrome tech too. Skyrim but in Night City, or something. This was my first CD Projekt RED game, and now I know full well why they are as legendary as they are.

The guns feel great, vanilla, DLC, or custom. The skill trees are unique, you can build the stats you want and find many ways to progress. By the end you're maxed out on everything if you know what you're doing, but you aren't punished for full-sending something.

Wanna go full netrunner with offensive quickhacks? Make like Dishonored and be a ghost that no one ever saw? Go in guns blazing with ARs and shotties and melee with some Berserk in your back pocket? Lure 'em all out with distractions and pick 'em off with silencers and snipers? Mix of all of it? Something else? Night City gives you that freedom if you grab it.

Until the end.

See, there's a handful of endings for the game. Everyone's gonna lean towards one of two. Temperance, where you let Johnny live a new life, and one he doesn't take for granted, because you, V, left an imprint on him. Or the Sun, where V puts it all on the line and achieves the goal he promised he and his best friend Jackie would get: Becoming a legend of the Afterlife.

Now depending on your choices, two other options open for you. You can sell your soul out to Arasaka, and help a samurai avenge his master, like a Devil would. Or you can choose to run away, escape Night City, and hit the open road with a clan of Nomads that treat you like family.

All of these are mutually exclusive to each other. What they aren't is mutually exclusive to everything else within Night City. Want to help the ones you meet? Take on gigs from every fixer from the oil fields to Pacifica? Win a few street races, a few bare knuckle brawls? See some saucy braindances? You can do all that and more. And that makes Night City feel alive. Like the beating heart it is.

And that's not even mentioning the little world within it. Dogtown, the storyline of Phantom Liberty. In essence, that story only gives V one ending and you have a 50/50 chance of even having it. Instead, you give someone else their four endings. You decide what happens to one Songbird. Do you send her to the stars? Send her home? Or do you choose to side with the NUSA and hand her over, denying her final request? Or do you honor that request and give her peace? Your choices decide what the Tower is.

This circles back to the endings. Of the five total (not counting the secret endings), none of them end well for V. Temperance? V is gone, lost into Cyberspace to give Johnny the body that selected him as it's owner. The Sun? Risking it all for a gig that could save your life. You're on a 6 month timer and it's counting down fast. The Star? Same gig. Riding out to Arizona and prayin' that those outside Night City have what you need. The Devil? Well, that one's your choice. Siding with Arasaka til the end, you're given a contract. Do you take the word of Takamura, and trust that when the time comes, there's a body waiting for you, but have no idea when or where? Or do you say to hell with it, and end your last 6 months of life on your terms? I know what I'd choose.

And then there's the Tower. The one ending that doesn't put V on a time limit.. but at what cost? If you make the right calls during Phantom Liberty, you have the choice to put your faith in NUSA to save your life. And they do.. but they leave you a mortal man. Not one capable of using cyberware or high tech guns or any fancy stuff. Just an ordinary choom on the streets of Night City. You survive, free to live YOUR life.. but was it worth it? Only you can pick that.

Of course, i'd be remiss not to mention your other option. You and Johnny can choose to end it all on your terms. Swallow a bullet and put an end to your story. Or, you and Johnny can go it alone. Strut in the front door of Arasaka Tower guns blazing. And this ties back to a question someone asks you in the game real early.

Live a long but quiet life? Or a short life that ends in a blaze of glory?

THAT is the beauty of Cyberpunk 2077. At the end of the day, it comes down to that question, that choice. And what you do with it and what you do to get to it.

What I picked, what I did, won't be what someone else did. And that's the point.

Every V carves their own path in life. As they should.

Play this game. You owe it to yourself to experience this story. You owe it to yourself to make it YOURS.

And heck, mod the game. I know I did. I was running around as a biosculpted exotic, with an early 60s Mustang at my call, and some extra firepower in my hands. And of course, with a car like that I had some proper music too. Blastin' some 2000s alt-rock and more. All what I would call preem.

An enthralling view into a unique and dystopian microcosm of our world with the freedom you want in an rpg.

This game builds a great dystopian world with a huge emphasis on a city and its destructive path it leads its residents on. Night City isn’t just a place for those seeking recreation and the future; it’s a comfort that slowly drains the people until it destroys them, giving them an ultimatum: rebel or die trying. There isn't a happy ending in this city and the game rams that in with detail. It provides a view that enriches your understanding and personal connection with the characters of the story, giving you choices that almost all lead to the same pre-determined ending. 
On top of the powerful narrative, the game provides immense character freedom and gameplay depth through its variety of builds. Additionally, the sidequests are engaging enough to keep you entertained throughout. It offers significant room for player expression, allowing you to play the game however you want.
Overall gameplay with a lot of freedom to express, and a captivating story that gets you thinking.

Cyberpunk 2077 delivers a rich and flexible RPG experience set in the breathtaking Night City. Rather than relying solely on its main story, it shines through its side missions, which are often just as meaningful and impactful. The central storyline is relatively short but engaging, driven by the relationship between V and Johnny Silverhand, whose presence adds tension and personality despite a somewhat stiff performance.

The game opens slowly, taking several hours to fully unfold, but once it does, it offers an impressive level of freedom. Night City is dense, visually striking, and filled with diverse locations, making exploration consistently rewarding. The structure emphasizes player choice early on, giving access to numerous missions that branch in different directions. While this can feel overwhelming and slightly disorganized, it also allows for highly personalized playthroughs.

Player decisions significantly affect both immediate outcomes and long-term story developments. Missions range from emotional to absurd, often blending tones seamlessly. Importantly, side quests are not just filler; they can unlock new endings and reshape the narrative in meaningful ways. The more time you invest, the more rewarding the experience becomes.

Character progression is flexible, allowing players to adapt their playstyle without heavy penalties. Combat evolves as you unlock new abilities, weapons, and cyberware, offering multiple approaches from stealth and hacking to aggressive gunplay. While not deeply complex mechanically, the variety keeps encounters engaging.

However, technical issues remain a major drawback. Bugs, animation glitches, and occasional performance drops can disrupt immersion, even on high-end PCs. While patches have improved stability, the experience is still not fully polished.

Verdict
Cyberpunk 2077 is an ambitious and deeply engaging RPG that excels in player choice, storytelling, and world design. Despite its technical flaws, it offers a memorable and highly customizable journey through one of the most detailed open worlds in recent years.

Petit préambule : je rédige cette critique après deux runs complets (DLC compris) effectués début 2026.

Cyberpunk 2077 a fait couler beaucoup d'encre et suscité beaucoup d'attente suite à sa présentation à l'E3 et la diffusion de multiples trailers alléchants. Lorsqu'il est sorti, de nombreux bugs et des finitions bâclées ont fait les choux gras de la presse et enflammé les réseaux sociaux. Pourquoi me sens-je obligé de faire ce rappel des évènements passés ? Tout simplement car on peut constater qu'en 2026, après de nombreux patchs et une v2 salutaire, Cyberpunk 2077 me paraît être pleinement à la hauteur des promesses faites à son annonce. 

D'abord, ce qui saute aux yeux, littéralement, c'est la beauté et la cohérence graphique du jeu. Il n'y a pas un recoin du jeu ou l'on ne sente pas le boulot et l'amour des artistes de CD Projekt. La moindre affiche, le plus petit bout de ruelle, le couloir d'immeuble le plus banal témoignent tous d'un sens du détail admirable. 
La partie graphique est également magnifiée par une surcouche technique bien musclée qui à grand renfort de ray tracing (ou path tracing) produit des ambiances lumineuses et colorées qui renforcent l'atmosphère des lieux. 

Ensuite, on est frappé par l'habillage sonore extrêmement touffu du jeu. Les longues promenades pédestres que l'on fait en jeu permettent de se sentir plongé dans cet univers grâce à de multiples couches de sons: des conversations, des jingles de pub, la circulation aérienne et automobile, des fusillades... Tout cela renforce l'immersion. 
Quant à l'ambiance musicale, elle est particulièrement soignée. Les nombreuses pistes musicales dispensées par les multiples radios thématiques sont toutes excellentes et raccord avec l'univers du jeu. La bande originale n'est pas en reste avec de nombreux morceaux illustrant efficacement les différentes phases du jeu. 

Le scénario nous met dans les chaussures d'un mercenaire débutant qui va au fil de ses aventures acquérir un statut légendaire jusqu'à un dénouement final qui ne manquera pas de vous surprendre ( plusieurs fins sont possibles, mais il n'y a pas de happy end à Night City). Toutes les missions, des principales aux secondaires, bénéficient d'un niveau d'écriture très élevé : il y a toujours des situations intéressantes, des personnages intrigants, des pans de lore à découvrir.
Les dialogues sont particulièrement vivants et n'interrompent jamais le flow du jeu. Les choix du joueur ont des impacts appréciables sur le déroulé des échanges verbaux. Mention spéciale aux choix de dialogues liés aux compétences ou background du personnage principal qui apportent vraiment une variété bienvenue lors de multiples runs.

Enfin le gameplay est vraiment satisfaisant. Les fusillades sont toujours excellentes, avec de nombreuses combinaisons possibles entre les armes, leurs modificateurs et les compétences du personnage.
Les capacités de hacking permettent vraiment d'enrichir l'approche tactique: utilisation des caméras de surveillance, explosion à distance d'éléments du décor, suicide des ennemis, affection contagieuse, cécité, surdité... La boîte à outils du joueur est très garnie et il n'appartient qu'à lui de se constituer son petit panier sur mesure d'armes, d' améliorations cybernétiques et de hackings.
Différents builds sont ainsi possibles, du ninja adepte des armes blanches au gros bourrin spécialiste des explosifs et du shotgun en passant par le hacker adepte des armes aux munitions téléguidées. Il n'y a pas un build qui n'apporte pas son lot de surprises bienvenues ce qui apporte une rejouabilité bienvenue. 

Vous l'aurez compris, Cyberpunk 2077 est un  jeu fantastique. Ce n'est pas les quelques bugs (jamais bloquants) ou les plantages (évitez le fast travel) persistant encore aujourd'hui qui vont vous empêcher d'apprécier ce qui est aujourd'hui à mes yeux l'un des plus grands monuments du jeu vidéo AAA moderne.

I finally put real time into Cyberpunk 2077, and honestly—it’s a completely different game compared to launch.

The biggest thing that stands out is how immersive Night City feels. The world is packed with detail, and just driving or walking around makes it feel alive. Between the lighting, sound design, and overall atmosphere, it’s one of the best-looking open-world games out right now.

Gameplay-wise, there’s a ton of freedom. You can go full gunner, stealth, hacking, or mix everything together. The combat feels smooth now, and the skill trees actually let you build your character how you want. Gunplay has weight to it, and cyberware upgrades make a big difference.

Story is where the game really shines. The main story pulls you in, and the side missions aren’t just filler—they’re actually worth doing. Some of them hit just as hard as the main storyline.

Performance has improved a lot too. It’s way more stable now, and most of the bugs that killed the game at launch are gone. It finally feels like the game that was originally promised.

That said, it’s not perfect. NPCs can still feel a little lifeless at times, and some systems don’t go as deep as you’d expect for a game this big. But overall, those issues don’t ruin the experience.

Final Verdict:
Cyberpunk 2077 went from a disaster at launch to one of the best RPGs you can play right now. If you skipped it before, it’s absolutely worth picking up.

One of the best games ever made, for a lot of reasons.

1. The Story
 - Masterfully done, never a dull moment when it comes to the plot of this game. The twists and turns will have you hooked and desperate to play that next story mission. 

2. The World
 - I believe this game accurately depicts a version of Mike Pondsmith's Cyberpunk. While a few small details are perhaps missing, cyberpunk fans will love this game.
 - THERE IS SO MUCH DETAIL. Everytime you play this game again you find something new, and it really shows just how much work the devs put in when building the world. 
 - You never run out of stuff to do. Even when you complete all the side quests and side content for this game, you can still find things tucked away in the nooks and crannies for you to explore. 

3. The Combat
 - THRE IS INFINITE BUILD POTENTIAL IN THIS GAME. Whether you min max. or play a generalist, pretty much any build you can think  of will be viable, and it always makes me want to go back and start over completely to try something new. You wanna play a tank that absorbs bullets and smashes gonks? You can do that. You wanna play a stealthy hacker that kills majority of enemies before combat even starts? You can do that. You want to be a total badass with a pistol and crazy  movement? You can do that. You want to be a tank, hacker, and total badass? You. Can. Do. That. 
 - The actual combat and gunplay in this game is soooooo unique. There's so many differentt ways to play. You can utilize cover and hit and run tactics with a stealthy build. You can bring a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ SWORD to a gunfight and utilzie crazy movement mechanics and hack away at gonks. You can do none of that, stare at people, and fry their brains before they can even unholster their weapon. Wanna be a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ cyber ninja with swords, throwing knives, and flys from enemy to enemy? DO IT. You wanna casually walk down enemies with a shotgun and (litterally) rip and tear through enemies like ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ doom guy?? DO IT. 
 - All in all, never a dull moment in this game when it comes to shooting people, or cutting them open...

4. The Progression System
 - As stated before, this game has INFINTE BUILD POTENTIAL, and my favorite progression system of all time. Leveling up in this game feels sooooooooo goooood. A single perk can change the entire way you play, and once you start getting a lot of perks, you can start killing a lot of gonks, with the master level perks turning you into a total cyberpunk.
- The attribute system is perfect. Opening certain areas or doing certian things in dialogue sometimes requires a certain amount of attribute points, litterally affecting your personality in game, which is CRAZY. 

5. The Side Content
 - All of the gigs are unique and are well put together. All of them have some story element that is interesting and sometimes funny, and a lot of times ♥♥♥♥♥♥ up, which is the cyberpunk way. I never feel bored when doing the side quests, ESPECIALLY in the Phantom Liberty Expansion, where everything is turned up to the max. 

This game had a terrible launch, and it's a realy shame, because this game could've had the potential to surpass super big games like GTA. This game has improved so, so so much. I would reccomend the game to everyone, worth every single dollar 10 times over.

Cyberpunk 2077 is a neon drenched body in a Capitalistic Dystopian world. The way V (one of the best written protagonist) tries to be a legend in a city that only cares about blood, money and sex is nothing sort of an epic in itself. And While the RPG gameplay at it's core gives you everything you need to enjoy this colored world. The actual soul of the game resides in the characters. From Johnny Silverhand to minor tells and encounter, all to make this world more lived in. It is a beautiful game with a beautiful DLC where every choice has a consequence but not in "Detroit become human" way but more like what you, as a player, feel way.

But it is not perfect, The most weakest point of the game itself is Romance section. While om witcher 3, even with minor scenes, Yen and Triss had an impactful presence. Even Shani was indirectly a great character. Here we have four choices, best being Judy and Panam. Now they are complex characters that you feel like, you wish to know about, Yet their questlines are small, interactions are quick and as soon as their stories completes. The rest of the game, they became a hollow shell of their previous self. And then Game pushes you to meet with them every day, Yet they act like NPCs left there with few commands to follow.

So I wish in the next game they work on all the characters more where you can have few but more impactful interactions with them.

So at the end, It is a triumph of storytelling, A magnificent world of bad endings and intimate but cynical soul. 
As one of the greatest game of these times, I highly recommend it.

I voted to recommend this game because I think there's a demographic/player type out there that would enjoy it and I don't want to discourage them from giving this game a chance. Maybe someone who loves a good mod, who can lose themselves in roleplaying out the world and character choices, who knows. 

PERSONALLY however I found this game hits a point where many elements I prize in a game: discovery, leveling up, upgrading, enemies hit a plateau, somewhere about 50% through the game. You start to see a limit to the guns/builds, a repetition in the enemy engagements, a been there done that to the quests, so on and so forth. I come from traditional Borderlands/Fallout/Skyrim esque open world plays where it felt like there were no matter how many times or long you've played, the game can find a way to surprise you. Not CP2077. I also found the emotional hook of the game quite shallow. I wasn't particularly invested in any characters' plight, least of all my own. The romancable characters were okay, rather one-note, and with limited interactions so if you wanted the immersion of V leaning on them for relief or escapism, you don't very far. By the final 90% of the game I found myself quick-tabbing through all the dialogue of the game in general.

As far as actual game function goes, seems as of 2026 the majority of the bugs are gone. Ironically in the first 5 minutes of cinematic some chars were T-posing, but other than that, everything seemed to work the way it was supposed to. Only 2-3 times over 80+ hours of play did I have to reload a game since an NPC wasn't moving to the next checkpoint.

It’s rare to see a studio double down this hard. Cyberpunk 2077 went from a cautionary tale about overhyped launches to a gold standard for how to support a game and its community. With the 2.0 update and Phantom Liberty, Night City finally feels like the living, breathing, neon-soaked nightmare we were promised. A true masterpiece in the end. Cyberpunk isn't just a game anymore; it’s a living project.

So far I've been really enjoying this game. 

[h3] Story and setting [/h3]

I've been positively surprised by the quality of the story and setting. I thought it might just feel like a mindless Nvidia GPU ad, but I feel like it has heart. I love the inspiration it's taking and showing from works such as those of William Gibson, Ghost in the Shell, Akira, the Matrix and Mad Max. As a fan of the cyberpunk genre this game really doesn't disappoint. You see familiar things everywhere and it really makes my heart sing. There are easter eggs related to other franchises, [spoiler] such as Portal [/spoiler], as well. You have futuristic vehicles, computer-controlled robot spiders, people entering "the net" or even being digitized. Futuristic weapons, AI and robot people. Japanese tradition mixed with future tech. Everything you'd expect from a cyberpunk epic. It's very nice.

I love Night City, it feels like a place set in the future. It's a sprawling city, where technology is always present. You see flying vehicles glide across the sky and holographic ads everywhere. The mega corporations dominate the scene with their huge buildings, not to mention how they dominate politically. So you feel the tension and dynamics between the crime-ridden streets, the police department, military, street gangs and corporate affiliates.

I love the characters. You have the super sweet Jackie Welles who invades your heart and all the love you see within his family. You meet street criminals, desert nomads, corporate people, netrunners, etc. All with their own personalities and desires. So I'd say the story feels immersive. I love how people make you a part of their lives, asking for help when they need it, inviting you to funerals when someone died, asking to talk when they're feeling down. It's fun to see what people have going on and to be a part of it. You're also often given dialogue options and it does give you a sense of agency although it's unclear to me how big of a difference the choices make.


[h3] Graphics and performance [/h3]

 I run the game with the RT medium setting at 1440p with FSR 2 set to Quality. But with RT shadows turned off. FPS vary depending on scene, most of the time laying around 50-60 fps. Sometimes it's over 60 too. While riding a vehicle it's typically 40-50 fps. I'm sad that I can't have RT reflections on, I feel like it would make a big difference visually, but it pulls my fps down to the 30s. :'( Maybe some day I'll upgrade my gpu and experience that. Personally I'm pleased with the performance, but I'm not very sensitive to fps. I can be really bothered by poor 1% frames but I feel like in this game the framerate is pretty steady at all times. Even if it drops a bit lower, at least it's steady instead of having sudden drops or jitter or anything like that. 

Graphically it looks great too. Sometimes I feel the uncanny valley of something looking photo-realistic, but typically you can tell it's a game. The lighting looks really good in my opinion. You can also tell the artists went all out putting lights and volumetric fog everywhere to add to the ambiance. I think it works. Bars and clubs feel atmospheric in their own futuristic way.


[h3] Sound design and music [/h3]

Speaking of atmosphere, the sound design is phenomenal. Right as the game is loading you hear these futuristic, digital-sounding noises that really set the tone. Then in the main menu you get a similarly futuristic atmospheric track and you already feel like you're being transported to another time, where people have robotic enhancements and have brain computer interfaces that they use to connect to the internet. Or at least I feel that way. The sonic atmosphere on the streets of night city feel like you're really in a futuristic town. You hear the people and cars, music from nearby restaurants, ads constantly blaring and the sound of flying cars buzzing somewhere above you. I love the music in the game. It's super nice to ride to the next mission while watching the scenery and listening to the radio in your car or bike. I also love the music during fights, it's high-adrenaline, pumping future music that really gets your heart pumping.


[h3] Gameplay and mechanics [/h3]

For me this game had a bit of a learning curve. It has you go through a bit of a tutorial in the beginning and to be honest the gameplay felt pretty intimidating for me. I did not understand how to be stealthy and get around or neutralize targets without being seen. So for quite a while I'd just get spotted, even in stealth missions, and end up in a big firefight. For quite a while I'd put off playing this because I felt intimidated and like I wasn't good enough. The game doesn't hold your hand just as much as some other games do. There can be more than one approach to finishing your objective and the game doesn't fail you for being spotted.

But over time I started to figure it out. It often does guide you through its design, even though I didn't notice it at first. Now I'm glad it doesn't hold your hand and gives you freedom to deal with things how you want. I also had to swallow my pride and accept using the quicksave and -load function. It's just a lot more fun that way. It frickin sucks to be spotted and blast your way through a stealth mission and feel like you messed up. Sometimes that happens because I expected something to work differently than it does in the game. For example in one mission you're looking for stached money in a casino. Even going near the door leading to the second floor suddenly triggers an all out gunfight from the guards. How was I supposed to know that would happen? But when you do manage to sneak around unnoticed and take care of guards silently, it's very satisfying. It's also actually not too difficult which feels nice. Sometimes MGS felt too difficult for me personally, this feels more like something I can handle.

I also felt intimidated by the abundance of menus. You have skills and perks, equipment you can wear, crafting, enhancements and consumables. It can feel overwhelming. But it's fun to learn new perks and see how it gives you new abilities to use when sneaking and fighting.

Anyway, I haven't finished the story yet and I don't know how far along I am. But so far I've been thoroughly enjoying it and am playing a bit every day. I love the variety in the missions you get and how you can at least seemingly decide how to relate to missions and different people. Too bad the story will end at some point, but I am looking forward to how it all ends. Wonder if it has multiple endings... Guess we'll see. :)

Cyberpunk 2077 is a game that borders on greatness but ultimately fails to achieve it. Nevertheless, I truly believe that everyone should experience this game at least once in their life. A truly unique experience that only CD Project Red could have produced

[Performance and Optimization]
 In its initial release, Cyberpunk was lambasted for its poor performance and optimization, as well as being riddled with bugs. In the current year however, Cyberpunk 2077 is a feast for the eyes and runs phenomenally. Additionally, in my current 47 hours of gameplay, I have only observed a single bug. This complete turnaround speaks to the dedication of CD Project Red towards their fanbase and they should be commended for taking the criticism and actively working to make the game better.

[Story and Environment]
Cyberpunk is often touted as an action role-playing game set in an expansive open world. While this can serve as an apt description for the game, in practice. this description falls apart. The main story and many of the side jobs  attached to primary characters for the most part, are extremely well done, with amazing writing, voice acting and animations that bring everything together in a neat package. Problems with the story arise whereby the player is presented with a multitude of choices and methods to complete given missions. However, there are no consequences for any player actions and to this extent, your choices ultimately don't matter.
  Night city is an expansive, visual spectacle but there is practically nothing to do in the city. If you go into this game expecting to have random NPC missions, dialogue and activities such as in Fallout or Elder Scrolls you would be greatly disappointed. NPCs on the streets are basically non-interactable with the majority having only single lines of dialogue. Missions are given via phone call, so all the game's important characters will just contact you to do missions as long as you attain the relevant level and street cred. As such, outside of finding collectibles and completing missions for steam achievements, there is ZERO incentive to explore Night City.
 
[Gameplay and Systems]
Gameplay is extremely fun with a variety of upgrades, weapons and vehicles that all feel great to use and experiment with. The game's systems are a bit complex at the start, but as you continue through the story and the open world, they are integrated seamlessly.
 The game's cyberware upgrades in the beginning are substantial but as you go further up the tree, they become mostly minor stat upgrades with no substantial effect on gameplay.
